articleTitle: High ambient temperature effects on the anthropometric status of the population: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
takeaways: For every 1°C increase in average temperature, children's weight-for-height scores decreased by 0.12 standard deviations and height-for-age scores decreased by 0.03 standard deviations, indicating measurable impacts on growth and nutritional status

High ambient temperatures showed an inverse relationship with anthropometric outcomes across 20 studies involving nearly 3 million participants, with children under 5 years old being particularly vulnerable to temperature-related nutritional impacts

In adults, elevated temperatures were associated with both underweight and obesity conditions, suggesting that heat exposure affects body weight regulation in complex, bidirectional ways rather than following a single pattern

Children in Sub-Saharan Africa showed the strongest evidence for temperature-nutrition relationships, indicating that populations in already hot climates may face compounded risks as global temperatures continue rising
